key_insight: Attention-Based Multiple Instance Learning can recover useful per-report signals from patient-level registry labels, turning routine operational data into training data for tumor group classification.
clinical_relevance: This could help cancer registries automate pathology report coding and reduce manual workload without needing large-scale new annotation efforts.
method: Used ABMIL to link patient-level labels back to the set of pathology reports that likely informed them.
Distilled a large noisy corpus into a smaller, higher-quality per-report training dataset.
Fine-tuned a classifier on the distilled data and compared it with established baselines.
evidence_signal: On BC Cancer Registry tumor group classification, the distilled model achieved a macro F1 of 0.83 and outperformed baseline methods across most tumor groups.
watch_item: Because the approach infers report-level labels from weak patient-level supervision, performance may depend on label quality, report consistency, and careful validation before deployment.
paper_title: Learning from Lost Provenance: Multiple Instance Learning for Cancer Registry Tumor Group Classification
